title: Combating the Emerging Ebola Threat in Africa
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition)  23 May 2026
content:
Ebola is a terrifying virus that kills between 30 and 50 percent of the people it infects. Fortunately, the world has been getting better at fighting it over the past decade. Since 2015, vaccines for Zaire, the most common Ebola strain, have successfully limited its spread whenever hotspots emerged in Africa. Genetic sequencing of that strain has allowed for rapid testing, which makes contact tracing significantly easier. Furthermore, African governments and NGOs have learned how to triage patients and have won the crucial trust of locals.

However, the latest Ebola outbreak is caused by a rarer strain called Bundibugyo, for which there is currently no licensed vaccine or rapid test. For months, it swept undetected through eastern Congo, exacerbated by aid cuts that meant fewer health workers were monitoring the situation. On May 17th, the World Health Organisation declared the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern. The initial tally of nearly 600 suspected cases and 139 deaths suggests this epidemic could be the worst Ebola emergency since 2018. To avoid a catastrophic death toll, urgent and coordinated international action is desperately needed.

To win this fight, scientists need strong incentives to swiftly develop and deploy a new vaccine. Donors, particularly governments in the developed world, must promise to buy ample supplies of a vaccine as soon as it is authorized to encourage pharmaceutical companies. While the WHO has noted that two promising vaccines are in development, it will be months before either jab is ready. Stopping Ebola also requires seamless coordination across different countries and robust funding for vital health organizations. Institutions like the WHO and the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention must be given sufficient expertise and financial support to lead the response.

Complicating the medical response is the fraught local politics of eastern Congo, which provides an ideal environment for an epidemic to thrive. The state barely exists in this region, and more than 100 armed militias continuously terrorize the local population. Much of the area where Ebola has been detected is controlled by the M23 armed group, which aims to topple the national government. Both the government and the militias have a history of prioritizing their own interests, severely hindering humanitarian access and the delivery of medical supplies. International mediators must aggressively pressure all factions to allow health workers and supplies to reach affected communities safely.

This current crisis marks Congo’s 17th Ebola outbreak in the past fifty years, and it will undoubtedly destroy many lives before it is contained. Fortunately, because the virus is not airborne, it cannot spread as rapidly as diseases like COVID-19. Nevertheless, this outbreak serves as a stark reminder of the urgent need to prepare for future pandemics. Robust vaccine research, the genomic sequencing of emerging viruses, and continuous disease surveillance act as the world’s immune system. When global commitment to these preventative measures is weakened, disaster inevitably beckons.
